tooltips.disabledFeature: Demo flow continues elsewhere. Try the highlighted button.
tooltips.voiceSandbox: Voice and Sandbox modes aren't in this preview.
tooltips.futureStage: Ian hasn't reached this stage yet.
tooltips.pastStage: Ian has already completed this stage.
tooltips.escRestart: Restart Ian's journey from the beginning?

presenterClickPath.action: Ian sends his wrap-up reply (input bar)

0.1.headline: Meet Miles.
0.1.subhead: An AI companion for Honda intrapreneurs.

D.1.milesMessage: Welcome back, Ian. Here's where the lawn mower project stands. What do you want to work on today?

milesMessages.text: Hey Ian, here's where your electric lawnmower project stands today. I pulled together everything that landed in your inbox, Slack, and project log this week. Before we pick a thread, what's the one thing you most want to move forward?

summaryCards.label: 3 emails
summaryCards.tooltip: Prototype feedback: runtime · swap-battery logistics · cut quality on tall/wet grass · weight & maneuverability · deck width vs. productivity · noise (positive). One tester got through about 2.5 properties before the charge dropped, so runtime is the recurring theme: a solo landscaper needs a full morning on one charge or a battery they can swap mid-route. The quiet was a clear win. Fix the runtime and the interest is there.

1.1.promptMessage: Where do you want to start? I've got a recommendation if you want one.

inputModes.label: Voice
inputModes.tooltip: Voice and Sandbox modes aren't in this preview.

inputModes.label: Sandbox
inputModes.tooltip: Voice and Sandbox modes aren't in this preview.

1.1.footerReason: Segment choice is your highest-leverage move right now. It shapes your pricing, channels, and proof points for the next 90 days.

ianInput.text: Honestly, I need to nail down who actually buys this before I build anything else.

1.2.ianMessage: I need to validate who my customer will be.
1.2.milesResponse: Smart place to start. Before we name a single customer, let's get sharp on the problem and who actually has it. Want to frame that out?

ianInput.text: Yeah. Let's start with solo landscapers and pin down the problem before I name anyone specific.

1.3.milesOpeningPrompt: Can you tell me a little bit more about what you're envisioning for the electric lawn mower?
1.3.milesIntro: Before we build, let's pin down the real problem. Five questions. Answer what you know, and flag what's still a guess. Which one are you least sure about?

questions.a: Gas mowers: loud, fumes, constant maintenance
Honda's brand will obviously win this

questions.hint: Point to evidence, not a feeling…

ianInput.text: I've been sketching the problem all week. Here's roughly who I'm picturing and where it breaks down for a solo landscaper.
ianInput.assetNote: REPLACE/CONFIRM PATH: Ian's hand-drawn Sharpie sketch (solo landscaper + gas mower, LOUD!!/fumes/always breaking, 2026 regs, crossed-out $?/battery?/too $$$).

sketchToForm.milesStructuringLine: Got it. Three of these came straight off your sketch. Let me line them up so we can pressure-test them.

fieldSources.note: not in the sketch; carried from earlier, stays flagged as a hunch
Ian crossed out the price/battery guesses himself; no evidence yet, stays empty

sketchToForm.milesClosingLine: The 'why you' I pulled from our earlier chat, and it's still a hunch. And you crossed out the price stuff yourself, so 'how do you know' is the real gap. Want to test it?

1.3b.milesIntro: Now the honest part. Which of these are hunches? I sorted your claims left to right. Anything on the left is still opinion. Want to defend one, or go test it?
1.3b.log: 3 hunches caught. Go talk to people before building.

ianInput.text: Yeah, the $2k price is the real hunch, I don't actually know if they'll pay it. I want to go test it, not defend it.

1.3c.milesIntro: You caught three hunches back there, all about what landscapers actually want and will pay. Before you build, you need evidence on those. Here are five ways to get it. Where do you want to start?

turns.speaker: ian
turns.text: I was going to ride along on a route first.

turns.speaker: miles
turns.text: Good instinct, and we'll do it, but a ride-along is richest once you know what to watch for. Right now you've got three open hunches about what solo landscapers value and will pay. Ten interviews answer those head-on. Do those first, ride along second.

turns.speaker: ian
turns.text: That makes sense, I'll start with the interviews instead.

typedExchange.note: Screen 1.3c is a SCRIPTED TYPED EXCHANGE, not a tap-the-card branch. The five method cards (steps) are DISPLAY-ONLY context. Ian responds by TYPING in the chat input: turn 1 (Ian) types his instinct and sends; turn 2 (Miles) replies with the pushback toward interviews; turn 3 (Ian) types his acceptance and a Send click advances to 1.3d. sendMode 'manual' = Ian's line types in (IanTyping) then the presenter clicks Send to commit each Ian turn; the final send navigates to advance.

1.3d.milesIntro: I checked our internal customer discovery tools, and Laura is the best fit to talk to first. She's a solo landscaper in Phoenix whose pain points line up with the problem you framed, and three of her clients have already asked her to switch to electric. Here's what you know going in.

personaCard.name: Laura
dayInLife: Up at 5 AM, four properties before noon
Truck, trailer, two gas mowers, blower, edger
Books new clients through Facebook and word of mouth
painPoints: First start of the morning wakes the whole neighborhood
Three clients have asked her to switch to electric
wouldPayFor: Same runtime as gas, won't accept a downgrade
whereToFind: Regional trade shows (4 in the next 90 days)

1.3d.milesFollowup: Ready to book that first call with Laura?

ianInput.text: Laura looks right. Let's book the call.

bullets: Picked Laura as the first interviewee
Built an evidence plan, starting with customer interviews
Logged hypothesis: Sole-entrepreneur landscapers will pay a premium for quiet, electric equipment.
1.4.milesFooter: Curious what you'll hear. When you're back from those interviews, we'll line up the colleagues who've built this kind of thing before.

checklistCheckpoint.note: After the 1.4 wrap, Miles introduces the checklist (use checklists.stages.spark.milesIntro) and the slide-over panel opens to the Idea Validation checklist. Also teaches Ian the checklist control exists in the top bar.

1.5.milesIntro: Hello again, Ian. Supply chain keeps coming up as your biggest unknown, and it's the thing that drives your cost. How do you want to tackle it?

ianInput.text: Supply chain's the thing keeping me up. If someone's already solved this, I'd rather talk to them than guess.
ianInput.voiceNote: Shown as a sent audio clip / voice memo, the one voice moment in the flow.

2.1.milesMessage: Welcome back. Your customer interviews are done, that's the willingness-to-pay question off your plate for now. What do you want to work on next?

recommended.name: Priya Okafor
recommended.title: Project Manager, Honda R&D
recommended.reason: Solved your exact problem: built the e-bike battery supply chain from scratch in 2022. Closest match, and she can save you about 6 weeks on the internal safety review.

recommended.name: Daniel Reyes
recommended.title: Operations Lead, Honda Manufacturing
recommended.reason: Knows the supplier landscape broadly, but hasn't done battery sourcing specifically.
recommended.inventedNote: INVENTED placeholder name, swap in a real contact.

recommended.name: Karen Whitfield
recommended.title: Honda alum, founded & exited a hardware startup
recommended.reason: Strong on fundraising and the seed conversation, lighter on supply chain.
recommended.inventedNote: INVENTED placeholder name, swap in a real contact.

actions.label: Browse directory
actions.tooltip: The full directory is not in this demo preview.

options.label: Connect with Priya
options.ianReply: Good point. Let's go with Priya.
options.milesReply: Agreed. I'll reach out and find time on your calendar.

options.label: Connect with Daniel
options.ianReply: I think I'll start with Daniel in Manufacturing.
options.milesReply: Daniel knows the supplier landscape, but he hasn't done battery sourcing, and that's the exact thing slowing you down. Priya built the e-bike battery supply chain from scratch, so she's the sharper first call. Want to move forward with her?

options.label: Connect with Karen
options.ianReply: Should I talk to Karen first?
options.milesReply: Save Karen for when you're prepping the seed conversation, that's her strength. She's lighter on supply chain, which is the thing in your way right now.

ianInput.text: The interviews made it clear pricing is the open question. I want to talk to people who've worked out the cost side.

2.1.milesContactsIntro: Then these are the people to talk to. I found three who've been through the supplier gauntlet. Here's how they stack up.

contactsGate.note: Do NOT render the recommended contacts or the branch on load. Sequence: milesMessage (Miles asks) -> Ian sends ianInput (pricing goal) -> beat -> milesContactsIntro (Miles surfaces contacts) -> THEN the three contacts + pick-and-respond branch appear. Priya must not be visible before Ian has stated his goal.

2.2.milesMessage: Good news. While you were at lunch, Priya got back to me, and I put a hold on your calendar for Thursday at 2 PM. Does that work, or should I find another time?

meetingCard.title: Project Manager, Honda R&D
meetingCard.context: She's already navigated the supplier and safety review process you're about to hit. She estimates she can save you 6 weeks.

actions.label: Suggest another time
actions.tooltip: Demo flow continues with Thursday 2 PM.

2.3.milesMessage: Good meeting with Priya. I've logged the key takeaways and added her to your network. Anything she said that you want to dig into before we move on?
takeaways: Priya's supplier contacts added to your Key Partners
Battery cost actuals revised: $730/unit (vs. your $620 assumption)
She can save you 6 weeks on the internal safety review

ianInput.text: The $730 battery actuals are huge. Update the model with her real numbers before anything else.

bullets: Connected with Priya, Honda's e-bike battery lead
3 supplier contacts from Priya's network added
Got Priya's real supplier numbers to update the cost model
2.4.milesFooter: Next question: do the lawnmower's numbers hold up under pressure?

ianInput.text: Feels good to not be guessing on cost anymore. What's left before the model's solid?

3.0.milesMessage: The Garage closed your biggest unknown. The Test Track is where we build the model and pressure-test it before the board sees it. I'd take you straight in, sound good?

ianInput.text: Before we dive in, what tools do I have for this stage?

3.0.milesToolsIntro: Fair. Here's your Test Track toolkit, the ones built for modeling and pressure-testing. Where do you want to start?

options.label: Business Model Canvas
options.ianReply: Let's start with the Business Model Canvas.
options.milesReply: Good call. The canvas is where the whole model hangs together. Let's lay it out.

options.label: Financial Sensitivity Model
options.milesReply: We'll get there, but the canvas comes first. Once the pieces are laid out, the sensitivity model has something to flex.

options.label: Devil's Advocate
options.ianReply: Can I jump to the pressure-test?
options.milesReply: Save that for once you have a model to attack. Lay out the canvas first.

options.label: Readiness Scorecard
options.milesReply: That's the last step before the board review. You'll get there, but start with the canvas.

options.label: Leadership Review
options.ianReply: Should I prep for the board now?
options.milesReply: Prep comes after the model holds up. Start with the canvas.

options.label: Prototyper
options.milesReply: That's for once the model holds up. Lay out the canvas first.

options.label: GTM Plan Generator
options.ianReply: Should I build the GTM plan?
options.milesReply: Go-to-market comes after the board says go. Start with the canvas.

toolsGate.note: Test Track ENTRY / tool-selection beat (in-flow). Sequence: milesMessage (Miles proposes going straight to the model) -> Ian auto-sends ianInput (asks what tools are available) -> beat -> milesToolsIntro (Miles surfaces the Test Track toolkit) -> THEN render the Test Track tools as the pick-and-respond options, styled like the Tools page shelf if possible. BMC is the recommended/scripted pick (presenterPath ['bmc']) and advances to 3.1; the others redirect (advance null) so Ian lands on BMC. This is the in-flow tools moment; the full Tools page (T.1) stays separately accessible from the dashboard.

3.1.milesMessage: Welcome back. You came out of the Garage with Priya's real supplier numbers, so let's get them into the Business Model Canvas. Your cost of materials still reflects your February draft, and her battery quote runs higher than you assumed (lithium's up since then). Want to plug it in?
3.1.milesUpdateIntro: Here's what's moved. Want me to plug in Priya's real numbers and update the model?

ianConfirm.text: Yes, plug in her numbers and update it.

3.1.milesClose: Updated. On your $2,000 price, that pulls your margin from 31% to 24%. Still workable, but 24% is the number the board will press on. The model holds together now, so the real question is how ready you are to defend it.

ianClose.text: Got it. So how ready am I, really?

bmcGate.note: BMC UPDATE beat (merges the old BMC flag + financial update). Sequence: milesMessage flags the Cost Structure block in the Business Model Canvas -> beat -> Ian auto-sends ianInput ('show me what's off') -> render the BMC grid with the Cost Structure section flagged -> beat -> milesUpdateIntro asks whether to update, shown alongside the comparison ($148->$172/unit) and updatedTable as the PROPOSED change (margin preview 31%->24%) -> beat -> Ian auto-sends ianConfirm ('yes, update it') -> ONLY THEN the model commits (margin lands at 24%, values settle) -> beat -> milesClose (names 24% as the board's pressure point, teases the readiness check) -> advance to 3.2. The financials must not read as 'Updated' until after Ian's ianConfirm.

3.2.milesMessage: The venture board review is next week. Here's the full readiness check: where you stand, and what they'll push on.

ianInput.text: Good. I'd rather hear the hard questions from you than in the review.

scorecard.label: Financials
scorecard.detail: 24% margin after the update, sensitivity still tight

3.2.milesScorecardLine: Three of five are solid. The two flags, your margin and the investment path, are normal at this stage. But the board won't stop there. Here's what a skeptical CFO opens with, and how you answer each.

boardQuestions.header: What if Laura's willingness-to-pay is wrong?
boardQuestions.challenge: Your 10 interviews skewed toward Lauras who already complained about gas. The broader landscaper market hasn't signaled the same urgency, and your price point sits 22% above the closest competitor.
boardQuestions.response: Lead with the willingness-to-pay signal from the interviews: seven of the ten said they'd pay at or above $2k once you walk them through the gas and maintenance savings. It's stated intent, not revealed yet, so name that openly and show the next step to harden it, a small paid pilot or preorder with a colder segment.

boardQuestions.header: You've only started 2 of 4 outside-investment conversations. Where's the funding path?
boardQuestions.challenge: The board needs a credible route to capital before it commits, and half those conversations haven't started yet.
boardQuestions.response: Two are already in motion, and Priya's network opens the other two. The ask is scoped to the next phase, validation through first revenue, with clear milestones, not an open-ended raise.

boardQuestions.header: Sole-entrepreneur landscapers are 12% of the market. Where's the scale story?
boardQuestions.challenge: Even if you win 100% of Lauras, that ceiling is too low for Honda's portfolio criteria.
boardQuestions.response: Laura is the wedge, not the destination. Show the adjacency path. Once the product proves out with sole operators, the case for commercial landscaping crews becomes much easier to make internally and externally.

3.2.milesClose: Every one of those has an answer, and you've got the evidence behind it: ten interviews with a clear willingness-to-pay signal, transferable supplier terms, and a clear wedge-to-scale path. The flags have plans. You're ready for the venture board review.

ianClose.text: Then let's get it in front of the board.

readinessGate.note: FULL READINESS CHECK (merges the old CFO devil's-advocate screen + the readiness scorecard; this is the Test Track culmination and it must RESOLVE, no branch). Sequence: milesMessage proposes the check -> Ian auto-sends ianInput ('run them at me') -> render the scorecard (5 rows, 3 pass / 2 warn) -> beat -> milesScorecardLine -> render the boardQuestions cards (what the board will push on, each with its scripted response) -> beat -> milesClose (resolves every flag, ends 'you're ready for the venture board review') -> Ian auto-sends ianClose -> advance to 3.5. No pick-and-respond branch here; the check completes and flows straight to the recap.

columns.label: Spark · Month 1
bullets: Picked Laura as the first interviewee
Built an evidence plan, interviews first

columns.label: The Garage · Month 2
bullets: Priya's real supplier numbers are ready for the cost model
Came away with transferable supplier terms

columns.label: The Test Track · Month 3
bullets: Financial model updated with current data

ianInput.text: Three months ago this was a hunch. Let's go.

T.1.subhead: Stage-by-stage. Use what you need, when you need it.

shelves.header: Spark · Ideate, test, and validate with customers
The Test Track · Model and pressure-test your work

T.1.milesCorner: Not sure where to start? Just ask, and I'll match you to the right tool.

inputModel.description: Every conversational screen shows a persistent input bar with three modes (Chat, Voice, Scratch Pad), always present like Claude's input bar. Forward motion comes from Ian appearing to respond in one of those modes, not from destination buttons. 'driver' = which mode is shown being used to advance this screen. Scratch Pad is used once (1.3), Voice once (1.5), Chat elsewhere. Three screens are branches: Ian can pick any option, Miles gives an honest tradeoff on non-ideal picks, and the screen advances only when Ian lands on the recommended pick.
inputModel.autoSendNote: MANUAL SEND everywhere. Nothing auto-proceeds between pages. Ian's scripted line auto-TYPES into the input bar (IanTyping), but it does NOT commit on its own: the presenter clicks Send to post it, and that send is what advances the screen. Branch screens advance on Ian's TAP. No screen navigates off a Miles message or a timer.

checklists.description: Per-stage progress checklists in a slide-over panel opened from a checklist control in the top bar. ALL stages are UNLOCKED and viewable so the intrapreneur can see upcoming tasks in every stage; the ACTIVE stage is expanded by default, the others are collapsed but expandable (NOT locked or greyed out). Scripted done/false state. Introduced as a checkpoint after the Spark wrap (1.4).

spark.title: Idea Validation
spark.milesIntro: Here's where Idea Validation stands. You've framed the problem and built the plan. What's left is the part that actually proves it, talking to customers.

items.text: Framed the problem from field sketch
Corrected the battery cost assumption with real supplier numbers
Follow up with the 2 other supplier contacts
Lock supplier quotes before the business case is due
Updated the financial model with real costs
Finalize the prep plan for the venture board review
